questions.question: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
questions.answer: == performs type coercion before comparison. 1 == "1" is true; 0 == false is true. === checks both type and value without coercion. 1 === "1" is false. Always use === in production code to avoid subtle bugs from implicit type conversion.

questions.question: What is "guard clause" pattern and why is it better than else chains?
questions.answer: A guard clause is an early return that handles invalid/edge cases at the top of a function. Instead of: if (valid) { ... lots of code ... } else { return error; } — you write: if (!valid) return error; (rest of function runs only in the happy path). This reduces nesting, makes error conditions explicit, and makes the main logic easier to read.

questions.question: Explain truthiness in Python vs JavaScript.
questions.answer: Python falsy values: None, False, 0, 0.0, "", [], {}, (). Everything else is truthy. JavaScript falsy values: false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN. Key differences: In Python, empty collections are falsy; in JavaScript they are truthy ([] and {} are truthy in JS). Python has no NaN truthiness issue since NaN is a float concept. Always know your language's specific falsy values.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Nested Conditions queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
